elements.sent-2411-001: This paper presents the first Open-Vocabulary Online 3D semantic SLAM pipeline, that we denote as \ours SLAM.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our primary contribution is in the pipeline itself, particularly in the mapping thread.
elements.sent-2411-003: Given a set of posed RGB-D frames, we detect and track 3D segments, which we describe using CLIP vectors, calculated through a novel aggregation from the viewpoints where these 3D segments are observed.
elements.sent-2411-004: Notably, our \ours SLAM pipeline is not only faster but also achieves better segmentation metrics compared to offline approaches in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-005: Along with superior segmentation performance, we show experimental results of our contributions integrated with Gaussian-SLAM, being the first ones demonstrating end-to-end open-vocabulary online 3D reconstructions without relying on ground-truth camera poses or scene geometry.

Our scene representation is composed of 3D instances with latent visual-language vector annotations inferred from the 2D input. At the end of the sequence the full map is recovered (Bottom: the left depicts open-vocabulary representation after mapping to the target vocabulary color-coded by the provided legend; the right depicts 3D segments colored by instances).} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-006: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) refers to the online estimation of a platform's motion, along with a map of its surrounding environment, from the data streams of its embedded sensors~\cite{cadena2016past .}
elements.sent-2411-007: While early SLAM research targeted robotics, where it is seen as a fundamental step for autonomy~\cite{durrant2006simultaneous , its wide industrial adoption came first from augmented and virtual reality~\cite{klein2007parallel and it expands nowadays other use cases~\cite{ebadi2023present,morlana2024topological .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Visual SLAM research, however, has mainly focused on geometric models, processing pipelines and optimizations~\cite{newcombe2011kinectfusion,whelan2015elasticfusion,qin2018vins,campos2021orb , and much less and mostly recently on the crucial aspect of the scene representation~\cite{rosinol2020kimera,rosen2021advances,tosi2024nerfs , that would further expand its potential for a wider array of tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Over the years, semantic SLAM representations have adopted many forms, e.g., object annotations in 3D point clouds~\cite{civera2011towards,yang2019cubeslam , objects as features~\cite{salas2013slam++,bowman2017probabilistic,nicholson2018quadricslam,wang2021dsp and semantic segmentations of point cloud maps or implicit 3D representations~\cite{mccormac2017semanticfusion,rosinol2020kimera,kundu2022panoptic,zhu2024sni .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: All of them, however, have been limited to a pre-defined closed set of categories, which narrows its applicability to real-world situations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Offline semantic 3D reconstructions have also been traditionally closed-set~\cite{bao2011semantic,kundu2014joint,kundu2022panoptic , but, after the development of CLIP~\cite{radford2021clip , there has been a surge of work on open-vocabulary 3D representations~\cite{Peng2023OpenScene,takmaz2023openmask3d,lerf2023,qin2024langsplat,nguyen2024open3dis,boudjoghra2024openyolo .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In the latter cases, however, the focus on offline processing limits their use in robotics, augmented or virtual reality applications.
elements.sent-2411-013: In this paper we present the first Open-Vocabulary Online visual SLAM (\ours SLAM).
elements.sent-2411-014: Our novel pipeline estimates, from a 3D point cloud representation, a set of 3D segments that are assigned a CLIP vector per segment.
elements.sent-2411-015: Specifically, our segments are initialized by back-projecting SAM~2.1~\cite{ravi2024sam2 masks, and are tracked by projecting and matching the 3D segments against the 2D masks.}
elements.sent-2411-016: The CLIP descriptor of th 3D segment is selected between the descriptors from the keyframes with better visibility.
elements.sent-2411-017: In particular, we also contribute with a novel model to extract per-instance CLIP descriptors from images before assigning them to 3D masks.
elements.sent-2411-018: In addition to being online and faster, our pipeline outperforms the segmentation metrics of offline baselines.
elements.sent-2411-019: As discussed earlier, our \ours SLAM is the first pipeline for open-vocabulary online 3D semantic reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-020: Unlike prior methods that rely on ground-truth camera poses or scene geometry, our approach integrates pose estimation and geometry reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-021: Table~\ref{tab:sota summarizes recent related works according to these aspects, with further details provided in the remainder of this section.. \boldparagraph{Open-Vocabulary Image Semantics.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Traditionally, semantic segmentation assigned pixels to a fixed set of categories~\cite{he2017mask, kirillov2019panoptic .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Seminal work on Contrastive Language Image Pretraining (CLIP)~\cite{radford2021clip , that encodes image and text tokens in a common latent space, revolutionized the field.}
elements.sent-2411-024: CLIP features can be classified into any category that language can express by computing their similarity to text inputs.
elements.sent-2411-025: Variations of CLIP improve its performance~\cite{zhai2023siglip, data-filtering-networks, cherti2023openclip, openclip_repo and the granularity of its features attempting to generate dense feature vectors~\cite{zhou2022extract, ghiasi2022scaling, sun2024alpha rather than per-image ones.. \boldparagraph{Open-Vocabulary 3D semantics.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Most approaches to open-vocabulary 3D semantics assume a known 3D point cloud and focus on the semantics.
elements.sent-2411-027: OpenScene~\cite{Peng2023OpenScene leverages OpenSeg~\cite{ghiasi2022scaling to compute CLIP features from images and trains a network to associate 2D pixels with 3D points.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: For each 3D point they perform average pooling on CLIP vectors from multiple views and then supervise an encoder to directly assign CLIP features to 3D point clouds.
elements.sent-2411-029: OpenMask3D~\cite{takmaz2023openmask3d selects $k$ views per object, crops its 2D SAM mask--similarly to LERF~\cite{lerf2023 --and computes a CLIP vector per mask.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: CLIP features are then average-pooled over each crop and view.
elements.sent-2411-031: Open3DIS~\cite{nguyen2024open3dis combines SuperPoint~\cite{detone2018superpoint with 2D instance segmentations and a 3D instance segmentator to generate multiple 3D instance proposals, describing each instance with CLIP features following OpenMask3D~\cite{takmaz2023openmask3d .}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: In contrast, OpenYolo-3D~\cite{boudjoghra2024openyolo uses a 2D open-vocabulary object detector rather than on 2D instance masks and CLIP features, classifying each object based on the most common class across all views.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Although this approach avoids extracting CLIP features, it restricts each scene to the initial set of predefined classes.

elements.sent-2411-035: LERF~\cite{lerf2023 embeds per-object multi-scale CLIP features within NeRF, enabling 2D image searches using language queries.}
elements.sent-2411-036: LangSplat~\cite{qin2024langsplat uses the Segment Anything Model (SAM)~\cite{kirillov2023sam to generate 3 levels of segmentation maps for each viewpoint, remove the background of each segmentation mask, and individually encode them to generate CLIP vectors.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: For each scene, CLIP features are encoded into smaller dimensional spaces, and a 3DGS~\cite{kerbl3Dgaussians representation is augmented with the reduced features to render novel viewpoints and query semantic labels on the decoded 2D rasterizations.}
elements.sent-2411-038: SAGA~\cite{cen2023saga builds on LangSplat to compute CLIP descriptors and incorporates affinity features optimized with a multi-view mask graph to cluster 3D targets; however, it has only been validated on 2D tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-039: LEGaussians~\cite{shi2024language , and Language-Driven Physics-Based Scene Synthesis and Editing via Feature Splatting ~\cite{qiu-2024-featuresplatting instead integrate 3DGS with both CLIP and DINOv2~\cite{oquab2023dinov2 features.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: While these approaches aim to learn 3D representations, their reliance on multi-point 3D-2D transformation (through rendering or rasterization) to compute 2D semantic features restricts their semantic representation to 2D, as evidenced by the lack of proper 3D evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-041: On the other hand, OpenNeRF~\cite{engelmann2024opennerf , optimizes a NeRF to encode the scene representation along with per-pixel CLIP features from OpenSeg.}
elements.sent-2411-042: OpenSeg features are projected into 3D to compute the mean and covariance of 3D points.
elements.sent-2411-043: The NeRF then renders novel views focusing on areas with high covariance to compute additional OpenSeg features and refine the model.
elements.sent-2411-044: Hierarchical Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Graphs for Language-Grounded Robot Navigation (HOV-SG)~\cite{hovsg first computes the full point-cloud of the scene from RGB+D using DBSCAN~\cite{ester1996density .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: It then applies SAM and CLIP to compute local 2D segmentations with corresponding CLIP vectors, which are projected to 3D and fused into the global representation using HDBSCAN~\cite{campello2013density,campello2015hierarchical .}
elements.sent-2411-046: They argue that relying solely on masked segments, as in LangSplat~\cite{qin2024langsplat , lacks critical contextual information.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Rather than computing CLIP descriptors like LeRF or LangSplat, they introduce a novel approach: three CLIP descriptors are computed for each mask based on (1) the full image, (2) the masked segment without background, and (3) the masked segment with background.
elements.sent-2411-048: These descriptors are then combined using a weighted average.
elements.sent-2411-049: To date, online semantic methods have focused exclusively on closed vocabularies.
elements.sent-2411-050: Semantic Fusion~\cite{mccormac2017semanticfusion was one of the first semantic SLAM pipelines, predicting per-pixel closed-set categories and fusing predictions from different views in 3D space.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Fusion++~\cite{mccormac2018fusion++ uses Mask-RCNN~\cite{he2017mask to initialize per-object Truncated Signed Distance Functions (TSDFs), building a persistent object-graph representation.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: In contrast, PanopticFusion~\cite{narita2019panopticfusion combines predicted instances and class labels (including background) to generate pixel-wise panoptic predictions, which are then integrated into a 3D mesh.}
elements.sent-2411-053: More recent works, such as those by Menini et al.~\cite{menini2021real and ALSTER~\cite{weder2023alster , jointly reconstruct geometry and semantics in a SLAM framework Additionally, NIS-SLAM~\cite{zhai2024nis trains a multi-resolution tetrahedron NeRF to encode color, depth and semantics.}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: NEDS-SLAM~\cite{ji2024neds is a 3DGS-based SLAM system with embedded semantic features to learn an additional semantic representation of a closed set of classes.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Similarly, Hi-SLAM~\cite{li2024hi augments a 3DGS SLAM with semantic features.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Like earlier offline methods based on NeRF and 3DGS, these approaches represent 2D semantics, with limited capabilities for 3D segmentation or precise 3D object localization.
elements.sent-2411-057: Figure \ref{fig:ovo shows an overview of \ours SLAM.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Given an input RGB-D video, a visual SLAM pipeline selects a set of keyframes ($\{ k_0, \hdots, k_n \}$ in the figure) and estimates their poses and a 3D point cloud representing the scene.
elements.sent-2411-059: From the 3D representation, our \ours mapping module extracts and tracks 3D segments and assigns per-3D-segment CLIP vectors aggregated from those extracted in the keyframes.
elements.sent-2411-060: We assume a parallel-tracking-and-mapping architecture, as first defined by \citet{klein2007parallel and adopted by most visual SLAM works~\cite{campos2021orb .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Our input is an RGB-D video \(\mathcal{V = \{f_0, \hdots, f_\tau\}\), \(f_\tau \in \mathbb{N _{\leq 255 ^{w \times h \times 3 \times \mathbb{R _{>0}\) standing for the RGB-D frame of size $w \times h$ captured at time step \(\tau\).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The SLAM front-end estimates, for \(f_\tau\), in real time, its pose in the world reference frame.
elements.sent-2411-063: The back-end selects a set of keyframes \(\mathcal{K = \{k_0, \hdots\, k_n\} \subset \mathcal{V}\) from which it estimates in an online manner the scene representation or `map' $\mathcal{M = \{ \mathcal{T , \mathcal{P , \mathcal{S \}$, composed by the keyframe poses $\mathcal{T = \{T_{0 , \hdots, T_{n}\}$, \(T_{n \in SE(3)\), a point cloud $\mathcal{P = \{_0, \hdots, _m\}$ and a set of 3D segments $\mathcal{S = \{S_0, \hdots, S_q\}$\footnote{Note that we use $\left(\cdot\right)$ for tuples, $\left[\cdot\right]$ for vectors, and $\{\cdot\}$ for sets.. .}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Every map point $P = \big( \begin{bmatrix x y z \end{bmatrix ^\top, \ l \big)$ is defined by its 3D coordinates $\begin{bmatrix x y z \end{bmatrix ^\top \in \mathbb{R ^3$ a discrete label $l \in \{-1, 0, 1, \hdots, i\}$, that indicates if the point belongs to any of the $(i+1)$ 3D segments of the map or if it is unassigned to any of them (\ie $l=-1$).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: Every 3D segment $S = \left( l, \mathbf{d , \kappa \right)$ is identified by its label $l$, its semantics are described by a CLIP feature $\mathbf{d}$, and stores a heap $\kappa$ saving the indices of the best keyframes in which $S$ is seen ordered by their visibility scores.}
elements.sent-2411-066: For every new keyframe $k_n$, we run an image segmentation model that returns a set of 2D segment masks $\tilde{\mathcal{S _n =\ s_0, s_1, \hdots\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: We then select the 3D points in $k_n$'s frustum, remove occluded 3D points and finally project the remaining points to $k_n$ obtaining the 2D point set $\tilde{\mathcal{P _n$, for which each point $p \in \tilde{\mathcal{P _n = \big( \begin{bmatrix x y \end{bmatrix ^\top, \ l \big)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: After that, we compute the mode \(m\) of $\tilde{\mathcal{P _n$ projected points' labels for every mask \(s \in \tilde{\mathcal{S _n\).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: If $m$ receives less votes \(v\) than a threshold $\epsilon$, we discard the mask $s$.
elements.sent-2411-070: If not, two possibilities can happen:.
elements.sent-2411-071: If mode \(m\) equals to $-1$, we will create a new 3D segment $S_{i+1}$ label $l=i+1$, an empty descriptor $\mathbf{d}$ that will be assigned later as described in Section~\ref{sec:sem_computation , and a keyframe heap, $\kappa = \{\left(n, r\right)\}$, initialized with $k_n$'s index and $s$' visibility score $r$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: If mode $m$ is $>-1$, we will assign the label $m$ to the 2D mask $s$ and every projected 3D point with label $-1$ will also be assigned the mask label, $l=m$.
elements.sent-2411-073: The keyframe index will be inserted into the heap \(\kappa\), and stored if it is one of the best views or if \(\kappa\) is still not full.
elements.sent-2411-074: After this procedure, the 2D masks that where matched to the same 3D segment $S$ are merged.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{3D Segment Mapper} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Function{3D\_segment\_mapper}{$\mathcal{P}$, $\mathcal{S}$, $k_n, T_n$} \State $\tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n\leftarrow$ segment\_keyframe$(k_n)$ \State $\tilde{\mathcal{P}}_n \leftarrow$ project\_point\_cloud$(\mathcal{P},T_n)$ \State $M = \{ \}$ \For{$s $ in $\tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n$} \Comment{For every 2D segment in $k_n$} \State $m, v \leftarrow$ get\_label\_mode\_and\_votes$(\tilde{\mathcal{P}}_n,s)$ \State $M = M \cup \{ m \}$ \If{$v > \epsilon$} \Comment{\#votes greater than threshold} \If{$m = -1$} \State $S_{i+1} \leftarrow$ new\_3D\_segment$(i+1, n, s)$ \State $\mathcal{S} \leftarrow \mathcal{S} \cup\{S_{i+1}\}$ \Else \State $\mathcal{S} \leftarrow$ update\_3D\_segment$(S_{m}, n, s)$ \EndIf \EndIf \EndFor \State $\tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n \leftarrow$ merge\_2D\_segments$(\tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n, M)$ \State $\mathcal{P} \leftarrow$ update\_point\_cloud\_labels$(\mathcal{P})$ \State \Return $\mathcal{P}$, $\mathcal{S}, \tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n$ \EndFunction \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:3dsegmentor}

elements.sent-2411-078: Similarly to HOV-SG~\cite{hovsg , for each 2D segment, we crop 2 images: one masking the rest of the image out, and another one with the minimum bounding box that contains the full mask (see an example in \cref{fig:clips_merger ).}}
elements.sent-2411-079: We then compute CLIP vectors for the full keyframe and for the two images that we cropped, and the final CLIP vector for the 2D segment is the result of fusing the 3 of them.
elements.sent-2411-080: Differently from HOV-SG~\cite{hovsg , that pre-tunes a set of weights on a specified dataset, we train a model to predict the weights for each dimension of the vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Specifically, it is composed of self-attention blocks between the three CLIP descriptors.
elements.sent-2411-082: Their output is flattened and feed to an MLP that predicts per-dimension weights for each of the three CLIPs in the input.
elements.sent-2411-083: Finally, the CLIP descriptor $\mathbf{d}$ for a 3D segment $S$ is selected between the 2D segments in the keyframes heap $\kappa$, as the one with the smallest aggregated distance to the rest.. \boldparagraph{Querying.}
elements.sent-2411-084: To query the 3D semantic representation, text queries are encoded to CLIP space using the template ``This is a photo of a \{category\}''.
elements.sent-2411-085: Then, we compute the cosine similarity between the CLIP descriptor of the query and the descriptor $\mathbf{d}$ of each 3D segment in $\mathcal{S}$.

elements.sent-2411-149: To address the limitations of using a fixed set of weights to fuse CLIP descriptors across diverse objects and scenes, we train a neural network to predict these weights based on the image content.
elements.sent-2411-150: To train the model, we use SAM 2.1 to compute segmentation masks on images and match these with their ground-truth 2D semantic labels.
elements.sent-2411-151: For each set of three CLIP descriptors, the CLIP merger predicts a weight for each dimension of each descriptor, merging them into a single CLIP.
elements.sent-2411-152: We optimize the model by minimizing the cosine similarity between the computed CLIP descriptor and the CLIP descriptor of the semantic 2D label corresponding to the input CLIP vectors.
elements.sent-2411-153: The model is trained for 15 epochs on the 230 scenes of ScanNet++, predicting only from the set of the 100 most common labels in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-154: We compare its performance and generalization against the naive approach of using only the segmented image~\cite{qin2024langsplat ; HOV-SG's approach; and our variation of HOV-SG's using three fixed weights.}
elements.sent-2411-155: The evaluation is performed on the 50 scenes validation set of ScanNet++ on the complete set of 1.6k labels.
elements.sent-2411-156: The results, in \cref{tab:clipmerger , show that our merging strategy outperforms the other alternatives.. \boldparagraph{Limitations.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Despite its good performance on 3D semantic segmentation, \ours SLAM performs a naive detection and tracking of 3D instances, that could certainly be improved.
elements.sent-2411-158: We also lack a system to fuse 3D segments for performing loop closure, as typically done by SLAM pipelines in long exploratory sequences.
elements.sent-2411-159: Furthermore, its current precessing time of \~ 1 frame per second limits its application to platforms with multiple GPUs available.
elements.sent-2411-160: Finally, note that our approach to train \clipours will bias it toward CLIP descriptors of objects classes, losing some of the generalization properties of CLIP vectors.
elements.sent-2411-161: Ideally, to maintain these properties, the training should be done on a bigger dataset, including both object classes and other semantic properties.
elements.sent-2411-162: In this paper we present \ours SLAM, the first open-vocabulary online SLAM with a semantic 3D representation, based on 3D segments described by CLIP features.
elements.sent-2411-163: We propose a novel pipeline to segment 3D points from 2D masks, and track them across different keyframes.
elements.sent-2411-164: Additionally, we developed a new approach to assign CLIP descriptors to our 3D segments.
elements.sent-2411-165: For each 2D segment in each keyframe, we compute a single CLIP descriptor by taking a weighted sum of CLIPs from the natural image, the masked segment and a bounding box around it.
elements.sent-2411-166: The weights are predicted from a deep model, which we show to be more effective than alternative approaches.
elements.sent-2411-167: We outperform offline baselines in both computation and segmentation metrics on the Replica and ScanNet datasets.
elements.sent-2411-168: We believe that our work, that bridges SLAM and open-vocabulary representations, opens both fields to a broader range of potential applications.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-169: This paper presents an Open-Vocabulary Online 3D semantic mapping pipeline, that we denote by its acronym \ours.
elements.sent-2411-170: Given a sequence of posed RGB-D frames, we detect and track 3D segments, which we describe using CLIP vectors.
elements.sent-2411-171: These are computed from the viewpoints where they are observed by a novel CLIP merging method.
elements.sent-2411-172: Notably, our \ours has a significantly lower computational and memory footprint than offline baselines, while also showing better segmentation metrics than them.
elements.sent-2411-173: Along with superior segmentation performance, we also show experimental results of our mapping contributions integrated with two different SLAM backbones (Gaussian-SLAM~\cite{yugay2023gaussian and ORB-SLAM2~\cite{mur2017orb ), being the first ones demonstrating end-to-end open-vocabulary online 3D reconstructions without relying on ground-truth camera poses or scene geometry.}}

elements.sent-2411-174: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) refers to the online estimation of a platform's motion, along with a map of its surrounding environment, from the data streams of its onboard sensors~\cite{cadena2016past .}
elements.sent-2411-175: While early SLAM research primarily targeted robotics, where it is seen as a fundamental step for autonomy~\cite{durrant2006simultaneous , its widespread industrial adoption stemmed from augmented and virtual reality~\cite{klein2007parallel .}}
elements.sent-2411-176: Today, its applications continue to expand into other domains~\cite{ebadi2023present,morlana2024topological .}
elements.sent-2411-177: Visual SLAM research, however, has mainly focused on geometric models, sensor fusion, processing pipelines and optimizations~\cite{newcombe2011kinectfusion,whelan2015elasticfusion,qin2018vins,campos2021orb , and much less and mostly recently on the crucial aspect of the scene representation~\cite{rosinol2020kimera,rosen2021advances,tosi2024nerfs , that would further expand its potential for a wider array of tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-178: Online semantic representations have taken various forms, \eg, object annotations in 3D point clouds~\cite{civera2011towards,yang2019cubeslam,fan2022blitz , objects as high-level features~\cite{salas2013slam++,bowman2017probabilistic,nicholson2018quadricslam,wang2021dsp , semantic segmentations of point cloud maps~\cite{mccormac2017semanticfusion,chen2019suma++,rosinol2020kimera or implicit 3D representations~\cite{kundu2022panoptic,zhu2024sni,li2024sgs .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: All of them, however, are constrained to a predefined closed set of categories, limiting their applicability in real-world scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-180: Offline semantic 3D reconstructions have traditionally used a closed-set approach~\cite{bao2011semantic,kundu2014joint,kundu2022panoptic .}
elements.sent-2411-181: However, following the development of CLIP~\cite{radford2021clip , research on open-vocabulary 3D representations has surged~\cite{Peng2023OpenScene,takmaz2023openmask3d,lerf2023,qin2024langsplat,nguyen2024open3dis,boudjoghra2024openyolo,tie20242 .}}
elements.sent-2411-182: Despite the good performance of these recent advancements, their reliance on offline processing limits their applicability in robotics, augmented reality, and virtual reality.
elements.sent-2411-183: In this paper we present an Open-Vocabulary Online mapping algorithm, \ours, which we also integrate into two different visual SLAM pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-184: See \cref{fig:teaser for an illustration of our online reconstruction results.}
elements.sent-2411-185: Our method estimates, from a RGB-D set of keyframes, a set of 3D segments that are assigned a CLIP vector per segment.
elements.sent-2411-186: The CLIP descriptor of each 3D segment is selected between the descriptors from its keyframes with better visibility.
elements.sent-2411-187: Furthermore, we also contribute with a novel model to extract per-instance CLIP descriptors from images before assigning them to 3D masks.
elements.sent-2411-188: In addition to being online and faster, our pipeline outperforms the segmentation metrics of relevant baselines.
elements.sent-2411-189: Unlike previous methods that rely on offline or expensive optimizations with ground-truth camera poses or predefined scene geometry, our online approach seamlessly integrates with simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-190: The introduction of Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP)~\cite{radford2021clip , which encodes image and text tokens into a shared latent space, revolutionized semantic segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-191: By computing similarity to text inputs, CLIP enables classification into any category expressible in language.
elements.sent-2411-192: Several variations of CLIP have enhanced its performance~\cite{zhai2023siglip, data-filtering-networks, cherti2023openclip, openclip_repo and improved feature granularity, aiming to generate dense feature vectors~\cite{zhou2022extract, ghiasi2022scaling, sun2024alpha rather than per-image representations.}}
elements.sent-2411-193: While closed-vocabulary methods outperform on predefined sets, open-vocabulary offers optimization-free generalization, highly relevant for diverse applications.
elements.sent-2411-194: With the advent of Neural Radiance Fields (NeRFs)~\cite{mildenhall2021nerf and 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)~\cite{kerbl3Dgaussians , semantics have become increasingly integrated into them.}}
elements.sent-2411-195: LERF~\cite{lerf2023 embeds per-object multi-scale CLIP features within NeRF, enabling 2D image searches via language queries.}
elements.sent-2411-196: LangSplat~\cite{qin2024langsplat applies the Segment Anything Model (SAM)~\cite{kirillov2023sam on each viewpoint to generate 2D masks, remove their background, and encode them into CLIP vectors.}}
elements.sent-2411-197: Features are then compressed into 3 channels and incorporated into a 3DGS~\cite{kerbl3Dgaussians representation, allowing 2D semantic segmentation.}
elements.sent-2411-198: Subsequent works build on LangSplat by incorporating affinity features optimized with a multi-view mask graph~\cite{cen2023saga or by integrating both CLIP and DINOv2~\cite{oquab2023dinov2 features ~\cite{shi2024language,qiu-2024-featuresplatting .}}}
elements.sent-2411-199: O$_2$V-Mapping~\cite{tie20242 combines CLIP and SAM within NICE-SLAM~\cite{zhu2022nice for an online reconstruction although only evaluating 2D semantics.}}
elements.sent-2411-200: The dependence of all these methods on multi-point 3D-2D transformations (via rendering or rasterization) for computing 2D semantic features limits their semantic representation to 2D--evidenced by the lack of proper 3D evaluation.

elements.sent-2411-201: Most open-vocabulary 3D semantic approaches assume a known 3D point cloud.

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{3D Segment Mapper} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Function{3D\_segment\_mapper}{$\mathcal{P}$, $\mathcal{S}$, $k_n, T_n$} \State $\tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n\leftarrow$ segment\_keyframe$(k_n)$ \State $\tilde{\mathcal{P}}_n \leftarrow$ project\_point\_cloud$(\mathcal{P},T_n)$ \For{$\left(s, l_s\right) $ in $\tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n$} \Comment{For every 2D segment in $k_n$} \State $mode, v \leftarrow$ get\_label\_mode\_and\_votes$(\tilde{\mathcal{P}}_n,s,\epsilon)$ \If{$v > \epsilon$} \Comment{\#votes greater than threshold} \If{$mode = -1$} \State $S_{q+1} \leftarrow$ new\_3D\_segment$(q+1, n, s)$ \State $\mathcal{S} \leftarrow \mathcal{S} \cup\{S_{q+1}\}$ \State $l_s \leftarrow q+1$ \Else \State $\mathcal{S} \leftarrow$ update\_3D\_segment$(S_{mode}, n, s)$ \State $l_s \leftarrow z_l$ \EndIf \EndIf \EndFor \State $\tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n \leftarrow$ merge\_and\_prune\_2D\_segments$(\tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n)$ \State $\mathcal{P} \leftarrow$ update\_pcd\_labels$(\mathcal{P},\tilde{\mathcal{P}}_n, \tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n)$ \State \Return $\mathcal{P}$, $\mathcal{S}, \tilde{\mathcal{S}}_n$ \EndFunction \end{algorithmic} \label{alg:3dsegmentor}

elements.sent-2411-312: In this paper we present \ours, an open-vocabulary online 3D mapping method, based on 3D segments described by CLIP features.
elements.sent-2411-313: We develop a new approach to assign CLIP descriptors to our 3D segments.
elements.sent-2411-314: The weights are predicted by a neural network, which we show to be more effective than alternative handcrafted approaches while maintaining generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-315: We outperform relevant baselines in both computation and segmentation metrics on Replica and ScanNet.
elements.sent-2411-316: We believe that our work, that bridges SLAM and open-vocabulary representations, opens both fields to a broader range of potential applications.
elements.sent-2411-317: For training, we use the top 100 semantic labels from the more than 1.6K annotated semantic classes, and evaluate on the whole set of 1.6K labels.
elements.sent-2411-318: ScanNetv2 has two sets of annotations, the original set with 20 classes (ScanNet20), and an expanded set with 200 classes (ScanNet200)~\cite{rozenberszki2022language .}
elements.sent-2411-319: We evaluate on the 5 scenes subset used by HOV-SG~\cite{hovsg (HVS), and on the whole validation set of 312 scenes (FVS).}
elements.sent-2411-320: Despite some overlap in physical scenes, ScanNet and ScanNet++ were captured years apart, with different trajectories and sensors, making images and reconstructions significantly different.
elements.sent-2411-321: Image blur and noisy depths make ScanNet more challenging than ScanNet++.
elements.sent-2411-322: For all scenes, RGB-D sequences have been rendered at \(1200\!\times\!680\).
elements.sent-2411-323: For Replica we use the common 8 scenes subset (office-0...4, room-0...2) with NICE-SLAM camera trajectories~\cite{zhu2022nice .}
elements.sent-2411-324: Our \clipours has a 5-layer transformer encoder with 8 heads and a 4-layer MLP.
elements.sent-2411-325: It was trained on ScanNet++ train set for 15 epochs, with batch size 512, on 4 V100 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-326: As pre-processing, we computed segmentation masks on images, matched these with their ground-truth 2D semantic labels, and pre-computed input and target CLIP embeddings to speed up the training process.
elements.sent-2411-327: Except when stated otherwise, we relied on SAM2.1-l for 2D instance segmentation, and SigLip ViT-SO400 for CLIP descriptors.
elements.sent-2411-328: We query the models with the set of classes of each dataset using the template ``This is a photo of a \{class\}''.
elements.sent-2411-329: For fairness in \ours evaluation, we reproduce previous approaches'~\cite{hovsg, engelmann2024opennerf, takmaz2023openmask3d, Peng2023OpenScene keyframes selection and querying.}
elements.sent-2411-330: We select as keyframes 1 every 10 frames.
elements.sent-2411-331: The representation is queried with each dataset's semantic classes, and each 3D segment is matched to the class with higher similarity.
elements.sent-2411-332: Following HOV-SG, the vertices of our estimated point-cloud are matched to the vertices of ground-truth meshes using KD-tree search with 5 neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-333: Profiling experiments were run on Ubuntu 20, with an i7-11700K CPU, an RTX-3090 GPU, 64 GB of RAM and 150 GB of swap.

elements.sent-2411-386: Observing OVO-SLAM matching results, we noticed some problems related with SAM's masks.
elements.sent-2411-387: When some 3D points are projected on the edges of a 2D mask to which they do not belong, they are wrongly clustered into it and matched to a 3D instance.
elements.sent-2411-388: Then, when these are seen again they will propagate the wrongly assigned ID.
elements.sent-2411-389: This phenomenon can be observed in particular on the edges of objects, where the depth and masks are less accurate, and masks propagate the ID of the object to the background, as seen in~\cref{fig:mask_bleeding .}
elements.sent-2411-390: To compensate it we developed two approaches:.
elements.sent-2411-391: First, we add a filter to only keep matches of 3D points that are assigned to the same object in two consecutive frames;.
elements.sent-2411-392: Second, we apply a low-pass filter to the depth map to mask the edges of the objects and avoid matching points around them.
elements.sent-2411-393: Results on ~\cref{tab:ablation_depth_kf show how while using the depth filter does improve the average mIoU, the limitation to match in consecutive frames does not.}
elements.sent-2411-394: As a consequence we keep only the depth filter although it does not completely solve the problem.

elements.sent-2411-401: Along with superior segmentation performance, we also show experimental results of our mapping contributions integrated with two different full SLAM backbones (Gaussian-SLAM and ORB-SLAM2), being the first ones using a neural network to merge CLIP descriptors and demonstrating end-to-end open-vocabulary online 3D mapping with loop closure.
elements.sent-2411-402: Semantic mapping targets the estimation of the category to which each element in a scene belongs, along with a consistent geometric representation.
elements.sent-2411-403: Rich semantic representations in 3D are essential for advanced robotic applications.
elements.sent-2411-404: Traditionally, semantic 3D reconstruction has relied on a closed-set approach in both offline~\cite{bao2011semantic,kundu2022panoptic and online~\cite{mccormac2017semanticfusion,rosinol2020kimera settings, including integrations into semantic Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) systems~\cite{civera2011towards,zhu2024sni,li2024sgs .}}}
elements.sent-2411-405: However, these methods are constrained by a predefined set of categories, which limits their flexibility and applicability in open-ended, real-world environments.

elements.sent-2411-406: Following the emergence of Contrastive Language-Image Pre-training (CLIP)~\cite{radford2021clip , there has been a surge of interest in open-vocabulary 3D representations \cite{Peng2023OpenScene,takmaz2023openmask3d,nguyen2024open3dis , including efforts in online mapping \cite{conceptfusion, gu2024conceptgraphs, yamazaki2024open —though not yet in full SLAM systems.}}}
elements.sent-2411-407: While these recent approaches have shown strong performance, their dependence on offline processing or ground-truth camera poses for mapping significantly limits their applicability in robotics, augmented reality, and virtual reality scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-408: In this paper, we present \ours, an Open-Vocabulary Online mapping algorithm, which we integrate into two distinct visual SLAM pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-409: An example of our online reconstruction results is shown in \cref{fig:teaser .}
elements.sent-2411-410: Our method processes RGB-D keyframes to generate 3D segments, each associated with a CLIP embedding.
elements.sent-2411-411: These segments are initialized by back-projecting masks predicted by Segment Anything Model (SAM) 2.1~\cite{ravi2024sam2 , and are tracked over time by projecting them into 2D and matching against new masks.}
elements.sent-2411-412: Each 3D segment's CLIP descriptor is selected from the keyframe views with the best visibility.
elements.sent-2411-413: Additionally, we introduce a novel model to extract per-instance CLIP descriptors directly from images, which are then assigned to the corresponding 3D masks.
elements.sent-2411-414: Our pipeline not only operates online and supports loop-closure optimization, but also outperforms existing baselines in segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-415: Our \ours estimates consistent 3D open-vocabulary semantics and seamlessly integrates with SLAM pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-416: Unlike previous methods that either use a closed set of categories, offline processing, 2D semantic representations or odometry.
elements.sent-2411-417: Several variations of CLIP have enhanced its performance~\cite{zhai2023siglip, cherti2023openclip and improved feature granularity, aiming to generate dense feature vectors~\cite{zhou2022extract, sun2024alpha rather than per-image representations.}}
elements.sent-2411-418: OpenMask3D~\cite{takmaz2023openmask3d selects $k$ views per object, crops its 2D SAM mask to compute a CLIP features, and then features are average-pooled across crops and views.}
elements.sent-2411-419: Hierarchical Open-Vocabulary 3D Scene Graphs (HOV-SG) \cite{hovsg relies on an offline hierarchical global fusion approach that requires precomputing 3D segments and features for all frames.}
elements.sent-2411-420: The authors argue that relying solely on masked segments, as in Concept-Graphs~\cite{gu2024conceptgraphs , discards crucial contextual information.}
elements.sent-2411-421: We adopt this strategy, and contribute by proposing a novel approach to learn the CLIP merging operation.. \boldparagraph{Online Semantics.}
elements.sent-2411-422: To date, online semantic methods have focused mostly on closed vocabularies.
elements.sent-2411-423: More recent works, such as those by Menini et al.~\cite{menini2021real and ALSTER~\cite{weder2023alster , jointly reconstruct geometry and semantics in a SLAM framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-424: Additionally, NIS-SLAM~\cite{zhai2024nis trains a multi-resolution tetrahedron NeRF to encode color, depth and semantics.}
elements.sent-2411-425: More recently, OpenFusion~\cite{yamazaki2024open and Concept-Graphs~\cite{gu2024conceptgraphs integrated open-vocabulary semantic descriptors into online 3D mapping pipelines.}}
elements.sent-2411-426: Concept-Graphs relies on the naive mask-cropping to compute CLIP descriptors, while OpenFusion uses SEEM~\cite{zou2023segment and creates a TSDF with 3D segments.}
elements.sent-2411-427: None of them, however, addresses the integration into a full SLAM pipeline with loop closure optimization as we do.

elements.sent-2411-512: In this paper, we present \ours, an open-vocabulary, online 3D mapping method.
elements.sent-2411-513: Our pipeline extracts 3D segments from 2D masks and tracks them across keyframes.
elements.sent-2411-514: To assign CLIP descriptors to 3D segments, we introduce a novel strategy: each 2D segment receives a single descriptor computed as a weighted sum of embeddings from the full image, the masked region, and its surrounding bounding box.
elements.sent-2411-515: The weights are predicted by a neural network, which outperforms handcrafted heuristics while retaining strong generalization.
elements.sent-2411-516: We also develop a mechanism to fuse instances that are affected by odometric drift after the geometric corrections of a loop closure.
elements.sent-2411-517: By bridging SLAM with open-vocabulary representations, we believe that our work broadens the scope of applications in these two domains.
elements.sent-2411-518: Following HOV-SG, the vertices of our estimated point cloud are matched to the vertices of ground-truth meshes using KD-tree search with 5 neighbors.
elements.sent-2411-519: We where unable to make OpenNerf converge in ScanNetv2, probably due to the impact of its noisy GT camera poses in NeRFs convergence.
